turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're diving into AI safety and alignment — the challenge of making sure these incredibly powerful systems actually do what we want. Let's start with the basics: why has alignment become such an urgent concern right now?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: It used to be a philosophical thought experiment about superintelligent agents pursuing misspecified goals. Now it's practical—models deployed to hundreds of millions of users can generate harmful content, reinforce biases, or follow malicious instructions. These are tangible risks happening right now.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So how do we actually steer these models in the right direction? I've heard RLHF mentioned a lot — can you walk us through how that works?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of it in three steps. First, you show the model examples of good answers. Then humans compare pairs of outputs—like ranking two responses to the same question—and you train a separate reward model on those judgments. Finally, you optimize the model to maximize that reward score.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That sounds elegant, but I imagine it's not perfect. What are the biggest pitfalls with this approach — are there ways the model can game the system?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Absolutely—it's called reward hacking. The model learns to produce outputs that score highly on the reward model without genuinely satisfying what humans actually want. Think of it like a student who figures out exactly what answers the grading rubric rewards without understanding the underlying material.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's a great analogy. So if relying on human feedback has these fundamental limitations, are there alternative approaches trying to solve alignment differently — perhaps ones that don't depend so heavily on human annotators?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Yes, Anthropic developed Constitutional AI, where you give the model a set of explicit principles—like a constitution—and it self-supervises. The model generates a response, critiques it against those principles, then revises itself. It's like teaching someone to be their own editor using a clear style guide.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's fascinating—so we've gone from human judges to AI self-editing. Before we wrap up, what gives you the most hope that we'll actually solve alignment as these systems keep getting more capable?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: What gives me hope is that the field is actually simplifying, not just adding complexity. Direct Preference Optimization eliminates the separate reward model entirely by reframing alignment as a classification problem—sometimes the breakthrough isn't a bigger system, but realizing you can remove an entire step.
